caveats: a: The occupation mix covers all NAICS 3119 rather than 311930.
a: AI opportunity excludes conventional batching and filling automation already deployed.
a: The injected compensation ratio is low and based on mismatched 2024 wage and 2022 receipt vintages, so small errors in task share can matter less than revenue volatility.
rho: No source directly measures five-year AI implementation for custom syrup manufacturers.
rho: Small firms may lack clean recipe and batch histories, while sophisticated contract manufacturers may adopt faster.
e: Public operator examples establish existence, not market share.
e: Several firms mix branded formulas, standard catalog syrups, and eligible custom production.
e: The small injected firm count means classification of a few firms materially changes eligibility.
s5: Owner-age data are for all manufacturing and data year 2020.
s5: Sale-intention evidence is Washington-specific and not industry-specific.
s5: One transaction can move observed rates materially in this small population.
q: No public source quantifies pass-through or renewal repricing for LMM custom syrup manufacturers.
q: Customer concentration can make nominal switching costs ineffective in negotiation.
q: Proprietary formula ownership by the customer reduces supplier leverage.
d5: Coca-Cola is a global proprietary system, not the LMM contract-manufacturing lens.
d5: Foodservice employment is an indirect channel proxy and does not map one-for-one to syrup volume.
d5: CDC health evidence creates policy and preference downside but does not provide a current volume forecast.
o: Regulatory accountability does not guarantee that the same independent supplier retains the work.
o: Consolidation into large concentrate systems or direct brand-owner manufacturing can reduce the lens population while an operator remains necessary.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B formulation and production programs priced per gallon, pound, bag-in-box, case, batch, or contract run, often combining product development, ingredient sourcing, blending, regulatory and label support, quality assurance, packaging, and palletized delivery.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 311930 includes both proprietary concentrate owners and independent outsourced manufacturers. The lens is narrowed to custom, contract-packed, co-manufactured, or private-label syrup and concentrate programs because these are the recurring external services specified by the default screen.
lens.excluded_activities: Global brand-owner concentrate systems, proprietary syrup brands without material outsourced-production revenue, captive bottler plants, finished ready-to-drink beverage filling classified elsewhere, coffee extracts classified elsewhere, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the approximately $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers that repeatedly develop, blend, quality-control, pack, and supply custom or private-label flavoring syrups, beverage concentrates, fountain bases, and related drink compounds for external beverage brands, foodservice operators, retailers, distributors, and bottlers.

a.bridge: BLS publishes occupations for broader NAICS 3119 Other Food Manufacturing, not 311930 or its custom-manufacturing subset, and does not measure AI task exposure. I treat blending, filling, sanitation, warehousing, and material handling as mostly physical, while assigning AI exposure to formulation search, specification and label drafting, production planning, procurement, quality-record review, maintenance triage, order service, and administration. The proxy may understate exposure where custom formulation and regulatory work are unusually large, and overstate it at highly automated bulk syrup plants.
caveats: The occupation mix covers all NAICS 3119 rather than 311930.
AI opportunity excludes conventional batching and filling automation already deployed.
The injected compensation ratio is low and based on mismatched 2024 wage and 2022 receipt vintages, so small errors in task share can matter less than revenue volatility.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit staffing and wage census plus time allocation from 15 custom syrup and concentrate plants covering formulators, QC and regulatory staff, planners, buyers, customer service, batch operators, fillers, sanitation, maintenance, and warehouse personnel.
a.rationale: In broader other food manufacturing, BLS reports packaging-machine operators (31,240), food batchmakers (30,470), material movers (16,180), hand packers (11,280), and production supervisors (9,850) as the largest occupations. Those physical roles cap exposure, but customer-specific recipes, claims, ingredients, and packaging add an information-work layer.

d5.bridge: Large-company and downstream indicators measure broader beverage demand, not constant-price quantity for LMM outsourced syrup services. Coca-Cola reported 2025 worldwide concentrate volume up 1% but North American unit cases down 1%; BLS projects food-and-beverage serving employment up 5% from 2024 to 2034, while USDA identifies ready-to-drink and nonalcoholic alternatives as growth drivers. I translate mature carbonated-soft-drink volume, modest foodservice expansion, and new beverage-format innovation into roughly flat to low growth for the current custom syrup and concentrate basket. This can overstate demand if health policy accelerates sugary-drink decline, and understate it if custom functional, low-sugar, and private-label programs gain share.
caveats: Coca-Cola is a global proprietary system, not the LMM contract-manufacturing lens.
Foodservice employment is an indirect channel proxy and does not map one-for-one to syrup volume.
CDC health evidence creates policy and preference downside but does not provide a current volume forecast.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year Circana, NielsenIQ, Technomic, and distributor physical-volume data for fountain syrups and beverage concentrates by format and sweetener, separated from price and finished RTD beverages, plus outsourcing-share forecasts from beverage brands and bottlers.
d5.rationale: Fountain and conventional soda concentrate demand is mature, but restaurants and nontraditional foodservice remain durable channels, and customers continue launching energy, functional, low-sugar, flavored-water, cocktail, and other formulations. The base is therefore modest rather than high growth.

caveats: Public operator examples establish existence, not market share.
Several firms mix branded formulas, standard catalog syrups, and eligible custom production.
The small injected firm count means classification of a few firms materially changes eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm classification of the 41 injected LMM-band firms using customer contracts, revenue mix, websites, FDA facility records, and interviews to identify the share of revenue from repeat custom, contract, or private-label programs.
e.rationale: Census defines 311930 broadly around fountain syrups and soft-drink concentrates, including proprietary systems. Public examples show independent firms offering custom formulation, private-label production, contract packing, wet and dry blending, and flexible runs. Because the injected LMM population excludes global brand owners but may still include regional proprietary suppliers, the outsourced subset is estimated at slightly under half.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311930 ($1,036,975,217) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311930 ($11,383,848,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $1,320,479 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311930); class <5: 53 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,479,264 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $225,588 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 27 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,156,519 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $328,869 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,446,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,135,566 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,541,094 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,285,017 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $144,230,643 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $21,995,173 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $605,118,571 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $92,280,582 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 41

o.bridge: FDA requirements apply to registered food facilities and do not directly measure operator-required quantity in 311930. Because custom concentrates require physical ingredient handling, controlled blending, testing, filling, traceability, and a documented food-safety plan, I infer that nearly all year-five demand still needs an accountable manufacturer. This may overstate retention by independent operators if customers insource or consolidate vendors, though software alone cannot produce and release the concentrate.
caveats: Regulatory accountability does not guarantee that the same independent supplier retains the work.
Consolidation into large concentrate systems or direct brand-owner manufacturing can reduce the lens population while an operator remains necessary.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews on insourcing and supplier-consolidation plans, capital announcements for captive syrup capacity, and audited studies of autonomous blending and filling that specify who retains legal food-safety and release accountability.
o.rationale: AI can accelerate formulation and automate records or controls, but it cannot replace the facility that owns the batch process, sanitation, verification, packaging, traceability, and release of a regulated physical product.

caveats: No public source quantifies pass-through or renewal repricing for LMM custom syrup manufacturers.
Customer concentration can make nominal switching costs ineffective in negotiation.
Proprietary formula ownership by the customer reduces supplier leverage.
q.change_evidence: Twenty representative customer contracts and renewal records showing term, recipe ownership, exclusivity, cost-plus versus fixed pricing, commodity and freight escalators, audit rights, productivity-sharing, minimum runs, rebid frequency, and margin outcomes after process improvement.
q.rationale: Customer-specific formulations, sensory approval, food-safety qualification, ingredient sourcing, bag-in-box or other packaging compatibility, and reliable batch performance create switching costs. Fixed per-unit or batch pricing can preserve benefits during a term, but large beverage and foodservice customers can rebid work, audit input costs, demand indexation, or capture productivity at renewal. Standard fountain syrup and simple blending retain less than differentiated formulations.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year AI implementation for custom syrup manufacturers.
Small firms may lack clean recipe and batch histories, while sophisticated contract manufacturers may adopt faster.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level before-and-after studies of AI-assisted formulation, scheduling, in-line quality prediction, maintenance, and compliance documentation, reporting labor hours, batch yield, changeover time, deviations, service levels, and recall or near-miss outcomes for at least one year.
rho.rationale: Formulation retrieval, documentation, scheduling, forecast support, procurement analysis, and quality-record exception review are implementable within five years. Realization is limited by legacy batch-control and ERP integration, recipe confidentiality, inconsistent customer data, process validation, label and allergen risk, and required human oversight of food-safety controls.

s5.bridge: A Census study reports 62.4% of manufacturing employer-business owners were 55 or older; Project Equity reports six in ten Washington business owners planned to sell within ten years but only 20% of businesses listed with a leading broker sold. I translate those cross-industry measures into a five-year completed control-transfer probability for eligible LMM syrup manufacturers, excluding closures, internal reorganizations, and minority deals. The broad age statistic may overstate aging in newer beverage-development firms, while broker completion may understate established profitable manufacturers.
caveats: Owner-age data are for all manufacturing and data year 2020.
Sale-intention evidence is Washington-specific and not industry-specific.
One transaction can move observed rates materially in this small population.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, and beverage M&A adviser data on closed control sales, failed auctions, closures, and the starting population of US custom syrup and concentrate manufacturers from 2021-2026.
s5.rationale: Long-established regional manufacturers create succession-driven supply, but the industry has only 41 injected LMM firms and sale intent does not assure transferability. A 25% base balances owner aging against process failure and strategic-buyer concentration.

principal_driver: Custom formulations and qualification-heavy physical production create repeat customer programs that software alone cannot supply.
principal_weakness: The LMM universe is small, eligibility is unmeasured, and mature sugary-beverage demand creates material downside uncertainty.

sources.supports: Census defines NAICS 311930 as establishments manufacturing flavoring syrup drink concentrates and related products for soda-fountain use or soft-drink manufacture.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition for Flavoring Syrup and Concentrate Manufacturing

sources.supports: BLS reports broader NAICS 3119's largest occupations as packaging-machine operators (31,240), food batchmakers (30,470), material movers (16,180), hand packers (11,280), and production supervisors (9,850).
sources.title: Largest Occupations in Other Food Manufacturing, May 2024

sources.supports: BLS describes food processing equipment workers as operating machinery that mixes, cooks, or processes ingredients and projects 5% employment growth from 2024 to 2034.
sources.title: Food Processing Equipment Workers

sources.supports: A US syrup manufacturer offers customer-provided or internally created formulas, blending, QC, private-label packaging, and batch sizes of 1,000-3,000 gallons across many syrup and concentrate categories.
sources.title: Sea Breeze Syrups Custom Pack Syrups

sources.supports: A beverage-concentrate co-packer offers private label, product development, wet and dry blending, fountain and frozen beverage bases, iced coffee and tea concentrates, bag-in-box filling, and small production runs.
sources.title: Consolidated Mills Manufacturing and Packing Services

sources.supports: A US supplier describes custom-developed flavors, private-label products, custom dry blending, contract packing, and concentrate, syrup, and powder-blend manufacturing for food and beverage customers.
sources.title: Grapette International

sources.supports: A Census working paper using 2021 ABS data reports that 62.4% of manufacturing employer-business owners were age 55 or older.
sources.title: The Metamorphosis of Women Business Owners: A Focus on Age

sources.supports: FDA requires covered facilities to implement written food-safety plans, hazard analysis, process and sanitation controls, monitoring, corrective actions, verification, supply-chain controls, and recall plans as applicable.
sources.title: FSMA Final Rule for Preventive Controls for Human Food

sources.supports: Coca-Cola reports 2025 worldwide concentrate sales volume up 1%, total unit cases even, and North American unit-case volume down 1% versus 2024.
sources.title: The Coca-Cola Company 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Project Equity reports six in ten Washington owners planned to sell within ten years, about 15% pass to family, one-third of owners over 50 have trouble finding a buyer, and only 20% of companies listed by a leading broker sell.
sources.title: New Research Details Effects of Silver Tsunami on Local Washington Economies

sources.supports: Coca-Cola describes US fountain-syrup sales to retailers, wholesalers, and bottling partners, and recognizes concentrate or syrup revenue when contract performance obligations are satisfied at shipment or receipt.
sources.title: The Coca-Cola Company 2024 Form 10-K

sources.supports: BLS projects food-and-beverage serving employment to grow 5% from 2024 to 2034 and expects more restaurants, especially fast-food and casual-dining locations, as population grows.
sources.title: Food and Beverage Serving and Related Workers

sources.supports: USDA FAS identifies increasing demand for ready-to-drink beverages and nonalcoholic alternatives as a primary growth driver in a broad category that includes flavored water and carbonated soft drinks.
sources.title: USDA FAS Trade Spotlight: Non-Alcoholic Beverages

sources.supports: CDC states frequent sugary-drink consumption is associated with multiple adverse health outcomes and reports that 49% of adults drank a sugar-sweetened beverage on a given day in 2011-2014.
sources.title: CDC Fast Facts: Sugar-Sweetened Beverage Consumption

sources.supports: FDA states 21 CFR Part 117 establishes CGMP, hazard-analysis, and risk-based preventive-control requirements generally applicable to registered food facilities.
sources.title: Current Good Manufacturing Practices for Food

title: Flavoring Syrup and Concentrate Manufacturing
